report_name: ShinyHunters' Extortion Attack on Canvas Cripples 9,000 Schools, Exposing Student Data

threat_actors.name: ShinyHunters
threat_actors.description: A notorious cybercrime group that executed a high-profile data extortion attack against Instructure's Canvas Learning Management System (LMS). The group claimed to have stolen 3.65 TB of data, defaced the login page, and demanded ransom from individual schools.
threat_actors.goals: Financial extortion of educational institutions by stealing sensitive student and faculty data and threatening its public release.

attack_patterns.name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
attack_patterns.description: The attackers exploited an unspecified vulnerability within the 'Free-For-Teacher' account functionality of the public-facing Canvas platform to gain initial access.

attack_patterns.name: Automated Exfiltration
attack_patterns.description: The group claimed to have exfiltrated 3.65 TB of data, suggesting an automated process to steal a massive volume of information from the compromised platform.

attack_patterns.name: Data from Cloud Storage
attack_patterns.description: Stole 3.65 TB of data, including 275 million records of student and faculty information and private messages, from the cloud-hosted Canvas LMS platform.

attack_patterns.name: Defacement
attack_patterns.description: The attackers defaced the Canvas login page, replacing it with a ransom note to announce the breach and state their demands.

vulnerabilities.name: Unspecified Canvas Free-For-Teacher Vulnerability
vulnerabilities.description: An unspecified vulnerability in the 'Free-For-Teacher' account functionality of the Canvas platform, which was exploited by ShinyHunters for initial access. The feature was permanently discontinued after the incident.

identities.name: Instructure
identities.description: The parent company of the Canvas Learning Management System (LMS) and the primary victim of the data extortion attack by ShinyHunters.

identities.name: Harvard University
identities.description: A major university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: Duke University
identities.description: A major university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: Stanford University
identities.description: A major university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: University of California
identities.description: A major university system impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: University of Southern California
identities.description: A university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: Tilburg University
identities.description: A university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.description: A university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.description: A university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

identities.name: Eastern Michigan University
identities.description: A university impacted by the ShinyHunters attack on the Canvas LMS.

indicators.name: Anomalous Canvas API Conversation Access
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Canvas API Conversation Access
id: 2b9c1d8e-4f5a-4b6c-8d7e-9f0a1b2c3d4e
status: experimental
description: Detects access to the Canvas API endpoint for messages, which could indicate scraping activity as seen in the ShinyHunters breach. This rule is intended for hunting and requires baselining to be effective.
references:
  - https://krebsonsecurity.com/2026/05/canvas-breach-disrupts-schools-colleges-nationwide/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-09
tags:
  - attack.t1530
  - attack.collection
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate application usage. High volume of false positives is expected without proper tuning and baselining of user activity.
level: low
logsource:
  category: proxy
detection:
  selection:
    c-uri|contains: '/api/v1/conversations'
  condition: selection
indicators.description: This indicator detects unusual or high-volume access to the Canvas API endpoint for messages (`/api/v1/conversations`), which could be indicative of data scraping activity.

indicators.name: Web Page Defacement via File Modification
indicators.pattern:
title: Web Page Defacement via File Modification
id: 8e7d6c5b-4a3b-4c2d-8e1f-0a9b8c7d6e5f
status: experimental
description: Detects modification of common web page files (HTML, JS) which could indicate a defacement attack, similar to how ShinyHunters defaced the Canvas login page.
references:
  - https://krebsonsecurity.com/2026/05/canvas-breach-disrupts-schools-colleges-nationwide/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-05-09
tags:
  - attack.t1491
  - attack.impact
falsepositives:
  - Legitimate website updates and maintenance. This rule is best used on production web servers where changes are infrequent and follow a strict change control process.
level: medium
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '.html'
      - '.htm'
      - '.js'
      - '.aspx'
  filter_known_paths:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\Program Files\some_legit_updater.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_known_paths
indicators.description: This indicator detects the modification of web page files (e.g., HTML, JS), which could signal a defacement attack like the one performed on the Canvas login page.

relationships.source_name: Web Page Defacement via File Modification
